one_line_takeaway: Small-budget (5%) word substitutions guided by embedding models -- especially a TM-AE-guided synonym+embedding hybrid -- collapse Fast-DetectGPT's AUROC from ~0.44-0.51 down to ~0.27-0.35 on XSum/SQuAD, showing zero-shot AI-text detectors are fragile to lightweight, interpretable lexical perturbation attacks.
key_finding: Embedding-vector-similarity substitution (Table 1) reduced Fast-DetectGPT AUROC from a baseline average of 0.9885 (white-box) / 0.9689 (black-box) to as low as 0.7269 (Word2Vec, white-box) and 0.7577 (TM-AE, black-box); BERT was the least effective embedding for the attack (0.7853 white-box / 0.8066 black-box), attributed to BERT's probability distribution aligning with the PLMs' own generation distributions. Increasing the disturbance percentage from 1% to 20% dropped Fast-DetectGPT AUROC from ~0.9 to ~0.6 on average (min observed 0.3428); selecting lower-probability synonyms (vs. more-similar ones) pushed scores from >0.95 down to <0.75. The hybrid synonym+embedding (TM-AE-guided) scheme was the strongest: across all nine detection methods, average scores fell to 0.10-0.20 (XSum 0.1282-0.1467; SQuAD 0.1438-0.1814); restricted to Fast-DetectGPT alone, XSum AUROC fell from a stated baseline of 0.4431 to 0.2744 (a 38.07% relative reduction) and SQuAD from 0.5068 to 0.3532 (30.30% reduction) — these are the two headline numbers repeated in the abstract and framed as beating the comparison attack of Huang et al. (2024, ToBlend). Across the nine-detector heatmap, Fast-DetectGPT and Entropy were the most attack-resistant (staying above 0.8 AUROC for several embeddings), while Rank was the most vulnerable (falling below 0.1 for many embeddings).
core_contribution: The paper (Khalid, Jiao, Shafik, Granmo — University of Agder / Newcastle University) proposes an adversarial text-perturbation attack that evades AI-generated-text detectors (primarily Fast-DetectGPT, plus DetectGPT, NPR, LRR, DNA-GPT, Likelihood, Rank, LogRank, Entropy) by replacing a small fraction of tokens (up to 5%, later varied 1-20%) in AI-generated text with low-probability alternatives drawn from word-embedding models. Three substitution schemes are introduced: (1) embedding-vector similarity (choosing low-probability alternatives directly from an embedding model's cosine-similarity neighborhood), (2) synonym-similarity substitution using WordNet-sourced synonyms ranked by embedding similarity, and (3) a hybrid two-stage scheme (WordNet synonym selection, then TM-AE-guided low-probability replacement of that synonym). The paper's own novel element is using the Tsetlin Machine Auto-Encoder (TM-AE), an interpretable/logic-clause-based embedding model, as one of six embedding sources, and using its transparent clause structure to make the substitution decisions traceable/explainable, contrasted with opaque LLM-based token-replacement attacks in prior work.
threat_model: The attacker has access to an AI-generated document and to word-embedding models (GloVe, FastText, Word2Vec, TM-AE, ELMo, BERT) plus WordNet, and wants to make a detector (Fast-DetectGPT or related zero-shot detectors) misclassify AI-generated text as human-written, evaluated under both white-box (detector uses the same PLM that generated the text for scoring) and black-box (detector uses a different scoring model, specifically GPT-Neo-2.7) settings. The attack budget is small: at most ~5% (in the main experiments) to 20% (in the sensitivity study) of words in each ~150-word document are replaced, filtered to exclude non-informative tokens and words outside the embedding model's vocabulary.
limitations: The paper does not report success/evasion rate at a fixed classification threshold, only AUROC shifts, so it is unclear how many individual documents actually flip from 'AI-detected' to 'human-detected' at any real operating point. It does not evaluate output text quality/fluency/semantic preservation after substitution beyond an informal claim that WordNet synonyms are meaning-preserving (no human or automatic quality/perplexity metric is reported). Datasets are small (500 XSum, 300 SQuAD, 500 Writing Prompts samples) and English-only. All embedding models were pretrained on the One Billion Word dataset with a 40,000-token vocabulary, which caps replaceable vocabulary and may bias results toward common words. Baselines used for the XSum 0.4431 / SQuAD 0.5068 comparison figures are attributed to 'similar work (Huang et al., 2024)' but the paper does not reproduce Huang et al.'s method itself under identical conditions in this paper's own pipeline, so the comparison is indirect (cross-paper numbers, not a controlled re-run). No defense or detector-hardening experiments are conducted despite the Ethical Statement proposing hybrid detection as future mitigation.

datasets_used: One Billion Word dataset (used to pretrain the embedding models, 40,000-token vocabulary)
quantitative_results: Fast-DetectGPT AUROC on XSum: baseline 0.4431 -> hybrid attack 0.2744 (38.07% relative reduction)
Fast-DetectGPT AUROC on SQuAD: baseline 0.5068 -> hybrid attack 0.3532 (30.30% relative reduction)
Table 1 embedding-similarity attack, white-box avg Fast-DetectGPT AUROC: baseline 0.9885 -> Word2Vec 0.7269 (best attack), BERT 0.7853 (worst attack)
Table 1 embedding-similarity attack, black-box avg Fast-DetectGPT AUROC: baseline 0.9689 -> TM-AE 0.7577 (best attack among low scores), BERT 0.8066 (least effective)
Disturbance-percentage sweep (Word2Vec + Fast-DetectGPT): AUROC drops from ~0.9 (1%) to ~0.6 (20%), min observed 0.3428, max 0.9719
Synonym-position sweep (min/mid/high similarity): AUROC drops from >0.95 to <0.75 when lowest-probability synonyms chosen, max 0.9974, min 0.5963
Hybrid scheme, all 9 detection methods averaged: XSum 0.1282-0.1467, SQuAD 0.1438-0.1814
baselines_compared: Fast-DetectGPT (Bao et al., 2024) as primary target detector
DetectGPT, NPR, LRR, DNA-GPT, Likelihood, Rank, LogRank, Entropy as additional zero-shot detectors evaluated

Huang et al. 2024 (ToBlend, token-level LLM-ensemble blending attack) and Sadasivan et al. 2024 (paraphrase-based attack) cited as prior adversarial-attack approaches and used for the XSum 0.4431 / SQuAD 0.5068 comparison baseline numbers
Six embedding models compared against each other as attack vectors: GloVe, FastText, Word2Vec, TM-AE, ELMo, BERT
